note: Non-military historical buildings and monuments only.

landmarks.name: Monument to Alexander Pushkin
landmarks.description: The central landmark of the street, depicting Pushkin in a dynamic pose on a red granite pedestal. First literary monument in Rostov-on-Don.
landmarks.fun_fact: Pushkin visited Rostov-on-Don in 1820 en route to the Caucasus with General Raevsky.

landmarks.name: Monument to S.M. Kirov
landmarks.description: Large brutalist dark stone monument built on the site of a demolished early 20th-century church.
landmarks.fun_fact: The original church on this site was demolished by the Bolsheviks in the 1930s.

landmarks.name: Monument to Anton Chekhov
landmarks.description: A statue of Anton Chekhov at the boulevard intersection bearing his name. Chekhov was born in nearby Taganrog.
landmarks.fun_fact: Chekhov spent his childhood in Taganrog, just 70 km from Rostov-on-Don.

landmarks.name: Monument to Vladimir Vysotsky
landmarks.description: Monument to the beloved Soviet poet, actor and singer Vladimir Vysotsky, one of several literary monuments lining the boulevard.
landmarks.fun_fact: Vysotsky's songs were passed on cassette tapes across the USSR before official release.

landmarks.name: Pushkin Decorative Spheres
landmarks.description: Large decorative stone spheres with bas-relief portraits and scenes from Pushkin's works, illuminated at night.
landmarks.fun_fact: The spheres glow from inside at night, creating a distinctive effect along the dark boulevard.

landmarks.name: Lyre Fountain
landmarks.description: A fountain featuring three large lyre sculptures on a low platform with water jets and evening light shows.
landmarks.fun_fact: The fountain is accompanied by a light and sound show during evenings.

landmarks.name: Library Fountain
landmarks.description: A light and music fountain in the grounds of the Don State Public Library. Key evening attraction.
landmarks.fun_fact: The fountain runs synchronized light-and-music shows on summer evenings.

landmarks.name: Don State Public Library
landmarks.description: Southern Russia's oldest public library, founded 1886. Holds over 5 million publications. Features a 16-story concrete book depository tower and 3-story reference wing with interior garden.
landmarks.fun_fact: Oldest book repository in southern Russia with over 5 million items.

landmarks.name: Rostov Regional Museum of Fine Arts
landmarks.description: The Petrov Mansion (1898) combining Baroque, Classicism and Renaissance elements. Houses 6,000 works of art including Bryullov, Aivazovsky and Repin.
landmarks.fun_fact: Many exhibits were looted during WWII German occupation and never recovered.

landmarks.name: Paramonov Mansion (SFedU Library)
landmarks.description: Grand neoclassical mansion for industrialist N.E. Paramonov with a portico of six Ionic columns. Now the Zonal Scientific Library of Southern Federal University. Federal heritage site.
landmarks.fun_fact: One of the finest examples of pre-revolutionary neoclassical architecture in southern Russia.

landmarks.name: Mansion of Pavel Kramer
landmarks.description: Merchant Pavel Kramer's mansion, now a restaurant. Fine example of early 20th-century merchant architecture.
landmarks.fun_fact: Kramer also owned the adjacent revenue house at No. 116, making it a merchant compound.

landmarks.name: Revenue House of Pavel Kramer
landmarks.description: Adjacent to the Kramer Mansion, this revenue apartment building reflects the merchant practice of building multiple income properties side by side.
landmarks.fun_fact: Revenue houses were the primary income source for wealthy Rostov merchants.

landmarks.name: Spielrein Mansion
landmarks.description: Childhood home of psychoanalyst Sabina Spielrein, pioneer of psychoanalysis and colleague of Carl Jung and Freud. Regional heritage site.
landmarks.fun_fact: Sabina Spielrein, who grew up here, was one of the first female psychoanalysts in history.

landmarks.name: Ivan Zvorykin House
landmarks.description: Built 1914 by Vasily Popov. Former office and residence of Rostov mayor Ivan Zvorykin. Three-story Art Nouveau corner building with openwork iron grilles.
landmarks.fun_fact: Ivan Zvorykin served as mayor during the city's most prosperous pre-war years.

landmarks.name: Gavala Residential House
landmarks.description: Late 19th-century merchant residential building. Regional cultural heritage site.
landmarks.fun_fact: One of many surviving pre-revolutionary buildings giving Pushkinskaya its merchant-era character.

landmarks.name: Suprunov Mansion
landmarks.description: Built by horse breeder I.A. Suprunov with imported Italian interior elements. Regional heritage site.
landmarks.fun_fact: Suprunov imported decorative elements directly from Italy for the interior.

landmarks.name: Lashch Revenue House
landmarks.description: Distinctive revenue house with Gothic Revival pointed arches and intricate brickwork. Rare style for southern Russia. Regional heritage object.
landmarks.fun_fact: Gothic Revival was extremely unusual in southern Russia, making this a rare architectural specimen.

landmarks.name: Mnatsakanova House
landmarks.description: Art Nouveau revenue house with flowing lines, wrought-iron balconies and decorative plaster. Regional heritage site.
landmarks.fun_fact: The interior reportedly preserves original marble finishes from the 1910s.

landmarks.name: Kushnarev Revenue House
landmarks.description: Revenue house by merchant V.S. Kushnarev near the intersection with Bolshaya Sadovaya Street.
landmarks.fun_fact: Located at the historically important crossroads of Pushkinskaya and Bolshaya Sadovaya.

landmarks.name: Reznichenko House
landmarks.description: Prime Neo-Baroque example from the late 1890s with elaborate stucco work and symmetrical compositions. Regional heritage.
landmarks.fun_fact: The elaborate stucco ornamentation required craftsmen brought in from central Russia.

landmarks.name: Bakulin Apartment House
landmarks.description: Late 19th-century eclectic building with Romanesque motifs. One of the earliest multi-family residences on the street. Regional heritage site.
landmarks.fun_fact: One of the first apartment buildings on Pushkinskaya, predating the major development boom.

landmarks.name: Bostrikiny House
landmarks.description: Art Nouveau apartment commissioned by Ivan and Anna Bostrikin in 1914. Restored 1997, now a Sberbank office. Regional heritage with irrationalist motifs.
landmarks.fun_fact: Color schemes from 1914 were restored during the 1997 renovation.

landmarks.name: House of Literary Figures
landmarks.description: Soviet-era building that housed poets Garnackeryan (1960-1977), Olenich-Gnenenko, and writer Sholokhov-Sinyavsky. Memorial plaque on facade.
landmarks.fun_fact: Three significant Soviet literary figures lived in this single building at different times.

landmarks.name: Greek Church of the Annunciation
landmarks.description: Single-domed Greek Orthodox church with parvis and bell tower, built by the Greek community of Rostov-on-Don.
landmarks.fun_fact: Rostov-on-Don had a significant Greek merchant community in the 19th and early 20th centuries.

landmarks.name: Rostov State Medical University Building
landmarks.description: Major institutional building housing faculties of Rostov State Medical University. Significant architectural presence on the boulevard.
landmarks.fun_fact: One of Russia's leading medical schools, tracing its roots to the late 19th century.

landmarks.name: SFedU Library Mansion
landmarks.description: Main library of Southern Federal University, housed in a historic neoclassical mansion. Provides access to global academic databases.
landmarks.fun_fact: SFedU is one of Russia's largest universities with over 50,000 students.

landmarks.name: Memorial to Nazi Victims 1943
landmarks.description: Memorial complex on the Don State Public Library grounds, commemorating over 1,500 Soviet citizens executed by Nazis in 1943 during German occupation.
landmarks.fun_fact: Rostov-on-Don was occupied by Nazi forces twice — in 1941 and again in 1941-1943.

landmarks.name: Bust of Semyon Budyonny
landmarks.description: Bust of Soviet cavalry commander Semyon Budyonny, associated with the Don region and the 1st Cavalry Army.
landmarks.fun_fact: Budyonny's 1st Cavalry Army was based in the Rostov region during the Russian Civil War.

landmarks.name: Monument to Mikhail Sholokhov
landmarks.description: Monument to Nobel laureate Mikhail Sholokhov, author of And Quiet Flows the Don. Most celebrated literary figure from the Rostov region.
landmarks.fun_fact: Sholokhov won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1965 for And Quiet Flows the Don.

landmarks.name: Historic Boulevard Lanterns
landmarks.description: Cast-iron lanterns in 19th-century merchant style lining the entire boulevard. At night they recreate the atmosphere of pre-revolutionary Rostov.
landmarks.fun_fact: Electric streetlights first appeared on this street in 1904, among the first in the city.

landmarks.name: Gorky Park Historic Entrance Gate
landmarks.description: Stone building and open-work fence at the main park entrance, designed by Doroshenko in 1893 in the shape of fortress gates. The park was established on former arroyos.
landmarks.fun_fact: The park was originally a city dump before being transformed into green space in the 1840s.

landmarks.name: Cathedral of the Nativity of the Virgin Mary
landmarks.description: Main Orthodox cathedral of Rostov Diocese, built 1854-1860 by Konstantin Ton (architect of Cathedral of Christ the Saviour in Moscow). Five-domed cross-shaped structure. Converted to a zoo in 1937, restored 1942.
landmarks.fun_fact: The same architect designed the Cathedral of Christ the Saviour in Moscow.

landmarks.name: Maxim Gorky Academic Drama Theater
landmarks.name_ru: Академический театр драмы им. М. Горького
landmarks.description: One of Russia's finest constructivist buildings, shaped like a caterpillar tractor. Located at Theater Square at the end of Pushkinskaya Street.
landmarks.fun_fact: The building's shape deliberately resembles a Caterpillar tractor as a constructivist tribute to industrialization.

landmarks.name: Gorky Theater Square Fountain
landmarks.description: Fountain designed by sculptor E. Vutechich with figures of giants surrounded by turtles and frogs. Destroyed in WWII and restored in the 1950s from surviving photographs.
landmarks.fun_fact: Sculptor Vutechich later became famous for the Motherland Calls statue in Volgograd.

landmarks.name: Underground Passage Mosaics
landmarks.description: Unique Soviet mosaics by master mosaicist Yuri Palshintsev in underground passages at main intersections, 1979-1984.
landmarks.fun_fact: Best photographed in late evening when foot traffic clears and lighting is optimal.

landmarks.name: Rostov Musical Theater
landmarks.description: The Musical Theater building resembles a white grand piano with an open lid. Rostov acquired its own musical stage in 1919; the current modern building opened in 1999 while the old 1892 premises became an architectural monument.
landmarks.fun_fact: Chaliapin and Rachmaninov both performed at the original theater on this site.

landmarks.name: Vorozhein Two-Story House
landmarks.description: Two-story historic residence by architect Doroshenko, one of the chief architects who shaped Pushkinskaya Street's pre-revolutionary character.
landmarks.fun_fact: Doroshenko was responsible for several of the street's most distinguished buildings.

landmarks.name: Boulevard Central Promenade (Alley)
landmarks.description: The central promenade alleys of lime trees, maples and chestnuts defining the character of the 3.45 km boulevard. The green canopy provides shade in summer.
landmarks.fun_fact: The trees were planted when the street was renamed Pushkinskaya in 1885.

landmarks.name: School No. 49 Historic Building
landmarks.description: School building from the 1910s recognized as a cultural heritage site for its architectural value from the early 20th-century construction boom.
landmarks.fun_fact: Built during the decade of most intensive architectural development on Pushkinskaya.
